does: This paper investigates how Low-Rank Adaptation (LoRA) changes internal representations in fine-tuned language models using sparse autoencoders as an interpretability tool. The authors train adapter-specific SAEs on Gemma-2-9B across multiple LoRA ranks (4, 8, 16, 32) and layers, introducing a delta activation framework to isolate the contribution of LoRA adapters to the residual stream. They evaluate representational divergence through multiple geometric metrics: cosine similarity between decoder directions, principal-angle analysis of feature subspaces, and Centered Kernel Alignment. The key finding is that LoRA-induced feature dictionaries exhibit comparatively weak geometric alignment with pretrained SAE features across layers and ranks, while adapter-specific SAEs reconstruct delta activations more effectively than pretrained SAEs, indicating that LoRA creates novel representational structures distinct from the base model's learned features.
connects: This work builds on the growing intersection of mechanistic interpretability and parameter-efficient fine-tuning. It extends prior SAE-based interpretability research that has sought to decompose neural network activations into interpretable features, while addressing the specific challenge of understanding how adapters like LoRA modify learned representations. The paper responds to limited prior work on LoRA's internal mechanics by applying tools developed in the SAE and feature geometry literature, including recent advances in alignment metrics like CKA and principal-angle analysis. It relates to broader research on representational changes during fine-tuning and the geometric properties of transformer representations, building on foundational work examining how adaptation methods alter model internals compared to frozen base models.
enables: This analysis opens pathways for improving adapter design and interpretability by revealing that LoRA creates geometrically distinct feature spaces from the base model. Future work could leverage these insights to develop more interpretable or controllable adaptation methods that either align better with pretrained representations or intentionally diverge in beneficial ways. The delta activation framework and adapter-specific SAE approach could be extended to other parameter-efficient fine-tuning methods, enabling comparative analysis of how different adaptation techniques reorganize learned features. Understanding representational divergence may also inform techniques for detecting, controlling, or exploiting unintended behavioral changes in adapted models, and could support development of safety mechanisms that monitor whether adapters introduce problematic representational shifts during fine-tuning.
